rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF HANS HERBERT BARTSCH
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Bar Respondent Arndt Peltner From Disputing That Decedent Hans Bartsch Was A Dependent Adult, Or In The Alternative To Re-Open Discovery As To Whether Decedent Hans Bartsch Was A Dependent Adult
rulings.Rulings: Motion to Bar Respondent Arndt Peltner from Disputing That Decedent Hans Bartsch Was a Dependent Adult, or in the Alternative to Re-Open Discovery as to Whether Decedent Hans Bartsch Was a Dependent Adult is denied. Herterich offers no authority that oppositions to a petition to determine distribution rights, filed as statements of interest under Probate Code § 11702, must behave like answers to civil complaints and generally or specifically deny each material allegation or else the material allegation is deemed admitted. Under Probate Code § 11702, "the written statement of each claimant shall be deemed denied by each of the other claimants to the extent the written statements conflict." Although neither of the oppositions to the Petition to Determine Distribution Rights specifically denies the factual allegation that Bartsch was a dependent adult at the time he executed the Will, both oppositions challenge the Petition as a whole and offer argument in conflict with the factual narrative presented in the Petition. The case law Herterich quotes is not on point: all of the cases but one feature writs to which absolutely no response was filed, and the remaining quoted case concerns procedures for motions for summary judgment, which is not relevant here. Herterich makes no compelling showing to support the Court to reopening discovery on this matter, in any event, and moreover offers no authority under which the Court could force an opposing party to file a statement under penalty of perjury stating the evidence supporting their defense to a petitioner's allegations, and before the petitioner has met his initial burden, as Herterich asks the Court to do. The Court will not sua sponte enter a judgment on the pleadings on this Motion, as Herterich requests in his Reply. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) and opposing parties no later than 4:00 p.m. Wednesday, January 30, 2019.

rulings.Case Title: THE ESTATE OF HANS HERBERT BARTSCH
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Second Motion To Use Settled Statement; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities In Support Of Second Motion To Use Settled Statement
rulings.Rulings: Motion and Second Motion to Use Settled Statement is granted. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) and opposing parties no later than 4:00 p.m. Wednesday, January 30, 2019.

rulings.Calendar Matter: ADDED TO CALENDAR FOR Hearing On Right To The Audiotape Recording (Set By Court)
rulings.Rulings: The request for the recording of Dr. Patel's examination is granted, subject to conditions. The examiner made an audio recording as allowed by CCP section 2032.530(a), albeit at the urging of parties who likely did not have the right to require that the session be recorded. Section 2032.610, while requiring production of a written report, is silent as to whether a recording--if one was made--should be provided. The Supreme Court in Gonzi v. Superior Court (1959) 51 Cal. 2d 586 concluded that the statutory provision for a written report would be inadequate to protect the parties' interest in an orderly procedure in the administration of justice that can test different versions of what transpired during the examination (and, therefore, ordered that a stenographer be allowed to report the session). That case dealt with concerns raised where the examining doctor was in the employ of a party and, therefore, not disinterested; whereas Dr. Patel performed an independent examination. Nonetheless, there may still be conflicting reports of what happened during the examination, especially given Mr. Suarez' cognitive limitations. An orderly procedure in the administration of justice is best served by allowing those who would challenge (or defend) Dr. Patel's conclusions to have access to the recording that he made. The Court recognizes that Mr. Suarez objects to production and that he has significant privacy concerns. Therefore, the audio recording and any transcript made from it may be provided only to attorneys for the parties and to experts engaged by those attorneys to evaluate Mr. Suarez' mental capacity, and those individuals may not disseminate the recording or any transcipript to their clients (other than Respondent Richard Anderson Suarez) or to anyone else without further order of the court. Any party wishing to contest this tentative ruling by appearing at the scheduled hearing must so advise other parties and the courtroom clerk (415-551-3702) no later than 4:00 p.m. Wednesday, January 30, 2019.
